sourceMovie.title: The Devil's Advocate
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: The Devil's Advocate works as a horror movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The horror and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Rosemary's Baby
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If The Devil's Advocate's horror stayed with you, Rosemary's Baby (1968) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The drama through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Primal Fear
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where The Devil's Advocate excels in drama, Primal Fear (1996) doubles down on mystery to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Case 39
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Case 39 (2009) is the natural next step after The Devil's Advocate: same horror DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and mystery elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Angel Heart
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of The Devil's Advocate who respond to its horror will find Angel Heart (1987) immediately familiar. Both films share a mystery sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Visitor Q
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason The Devil's Advocate fans keep landing on Visitor Q (2001) — the horror is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Nefarious
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Nefarious draws from the same horror well as The Devil's Advocate, channeling drama in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2023 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Puncture
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Puncture (2011) captures the drama appeal of The Devil's Advocate without simply imitating it. The mystery streak running through both films is what puts Puncture at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Se7en
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The mystery appeal of The Devil's Advocate — that dark edge, the crime — translates cleanly into Se7en (1995). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Blow
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Blow (2001) clearly understood what makes drama work — the same quality that keeps The Devil's Advocate rewatchable. Expect the same tense rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Revolutionary Road
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Revolutionary Road (2008) is the kind of film The Devil's Advocate fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The drama overlap is real, but it's the bittersweet emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

faq.question: What should I watch after The Devil's Advocate?
faq.answer: Try Rosemary's Baby, Primal Fear, Case 39—each shares DNA with The Devil's Advocate while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is The Devil's Advocate?
faq.answer: The Devil's Advocate (1997) is best described as Horror, Drama, Mystery. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is The Devil's Advocate worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.5/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this horror lane.
